host-2023-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'American Folk Music' paragraph says the music traveled from the British Isles to America 'in ballads and hymns, the words changing into new songs but the music immutable' — the tunes were kept, the lyrics were rewritten, which is exactly what A states.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the text says about the RELATIONSHIP between British and American folk songs — so you are looking for a claim about how the two are connected. With relationship questions, expect one of four shapes: kept-and-changed, fully-changed, fully-kept, or lost-the-link. Skim the options first to confirm which axis is being tested.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: One sentence carries the whole claim: 'It traveled down the Appalachian – via fiddle, banjo and guitar – in ballads and hymns, the words changing into new songs but the music immutable.' Everything else in the paragraph is set-up (where it came from) or example (Woody Guthrie's mother).

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: as the music traveled, two things happened in opposite directions. The lyrics changed — old British words turned into new American songs. The melodies stayed the same — 'immutable' means unchangeable. So British tune + new American words = the American folk repertoire.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Immutable' is the key word — it literally means cannot be changed (Latin im- 'not' + mutare 'to change'). 'Ballads and hymns' are types of song; 'Anglo-Celtic' just labels the British-Isles origin. None of this changes the headline: melodies kept, words rewritten.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: A ('melodies retained, lyrics replaced') is the paraphrase of 'words changing into new songs but the music immutable' — exact fit. B says they 'developed beyond recognition' — but the music was immutable, so this contradicts the text. C says American music 'lost touch' with its British roots — the Woody Guthrie example shows the opposite. D flips the polarity: 'British lyrics with new music' — the text says new words, old music.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The whole paragraph hinges on the single contrast in one sentence: words mutable, music immutable. Insight in one sentence: when a passage gives you a paired contrast inside one sentence, the right answer almost always echoes that exact pairing.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to see 'words changing into new songs' and infer that the songs changed beyond recognition — 'changing' and 'beyond recognition' feel close.
distractors.why_wrong: The text qualifies the change: words changed, music did NOT. Step 3's paraphrase splits the two halves of the sentence; B collapses them into a single 'unrecognisable' claim the text never makes.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the line about songs traveling from Britain and becoming American and assume the music drifted away from its origins over generations.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says the opposite — the music was 'immutable' and Woody Guthrie's mother was still singing 'Anglo-Celtic ballads' in Oklahoma. Step 2's quote shows the British link was preserved, not lost.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the contrast as 'something kept, something new' but flip which is which, D feels right — British input + American output.
distractors.why_wrong: D inverts the polarity. The text says the WORDS changed (new lyrics) and the MUSIC stayed (old melodies). D pairs old lyrics with new music — the exact mirror of what the passage states. Step 5's option-by-option match catches this.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-031.technique: Paired-contrast scanning: when a passage builds its central claim through a single 'X but Y' sentence (here, 'words changing... music immutable'), the correct option almost always mirrors that exact pairing. Trigger to memorise: 'comma + but' inside a sentence about the topic the question asks about — that's your headline.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Polarity-flip traps. Two of the four distractors here swap which element changed and which stayed. The cure: before matching, write the contrast as 'X kept, Y changed' on the side, then check each option matches both halves in the right order.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The Great Lakes paragraph frames the imported Asian carp and quagga mussel as 'blunders' that 'sometimes stemmed from well-meaning policies' — the damage came from interventions intended to help, which is exactly what D says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: 'What is implied here?' is one of the looser prompts on the ELF — it's asking you to draw a CONCLUSION that follows from the paragraph's claims without being stated word-for-word. Treat it like a 'main inference' question: find the one idea that ties the examples together.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two phrasings carry the implication. The first: 'Blunders sometimes stemmed from well-meaning policies.' The second: 'Researchers imported Asian carp to kill river nuisances without chemicals, and now some worry the fish has silently invaded Lake Michigan's floor.' The carp was deliberately introduced for a good reason — and it backfired.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: humans tried to fix a problem (river pests, chemical pollution) by importing a foreign fish, and the fix became a worse problem. The paragraph generalises this — 'blunders sometimes stemmed from well-meaning policies' — so the pattern is: good intentions, bad ecological outcome.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Blunders' = clumsy mistakes; 'stemmed from' = originated in; 'well-meaning' = intending to do good. So 'well-meaning policies' means policies adopted with good intentions. 'Invasive species' means a species that has spread into an environment where it didn't originally live and now harms it.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Look at each option against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'good intentions caused damage'. The option that says some of the environmental damage was unintended — caused by attempts to solve other problems — fits the 'well-meaning policies' line exactly. Any option that blames pure neglect, or that says nothing can be done, doesn't match the 'well-meaning' qualifier.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The Asian-carp example and the 'well-meaning policies' sentence together imply that some of the lakes' troubles came from human efforts that were intended to help but produced unintended consequences. Insight in one sentence: when a passage names something as a 'blunder' and immediately explains the good intention behind it, the implied claim is always 'unintended consequence', not 'wilful damage'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'invasive species first snuck up through the man-made Saint Lawrence Seaway' and conclude that the Seaway is the single cause of everything — a tidy origin story.
distractors.why_wrong: The Seaway is one entry point, not the whole explanation. The carp was deliberately imported (not snuck) and the quagga mussel has spread far beyond the Seaway's reach. Step 2's second key sentence shows multiple causal paths, not one.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'an ecological catastrophe unlike any this continent has seen' and read in a note of hopelessness — if the catastrophe is unprecedented, surely it cannot be fixed.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes the scale of the damage but never claims it is irreversible. B imports an idea — 'too late to act' — that the paragraph does not make. Step 3's paraphrase has no 'no hope' element.

distractors.why_tempting: Left-to-right reading of 'quagga mussel that has infested regions as far away as Lake Mead' suggests the threat is moving outward, so the answer must be about wider geographic spread.
distractors.why_wrong: Geographic spread is one detail, but the question asks what is IMPLIED — and the implication is structural (causes), not geographic (extent). Step 1 reminds you that implication questions look for the unifying idea, not the most striking image.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-032.technique: Implied-meaning scanning: when an ELF prompt asks 'what is implied?', you're hunting for the one generalisation that ties multiple examples together. Read the paragraph's framing sentence ('Blunders sometimes stemmed from well-meaning policies') as the thesis statement — the implied answer will paraphrase it. Trigger to memorise: 'implied' = look for the umbrella claim, not the loudest example.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: Vivid-example bait. The carp and the mussel are concrete and memorable, so distractors that name those specifics feel safe. The cure: distinguish framing sentences (claims about the whole topic) from example sentences (illustrations of the claim). The implied answer always rides the framing.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The author piles on statistics ('one in five adults', '81 percent of American kids', 'tripled', '40-fold') and labels them 'alarming' before calling the situation a 'dystopia of overdiagnosed mental illness' — the numbers are presented as evidence that something has gone wrong, which matches D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the AUTHOR'S VIEW of the statistics in the first few paragraphs — not what the statistics are, but how the author frames them. With author-view questions, the right answer is signalled by adjectives, evaluative verbs, and the conclusion the author draws right after the numbers.

steps.title: Locate the framing words
steps.text: Three pieces of framing matter. Opening: 'a dystopia of overdiagnosed mental illness'. Mid-paragraph: 'The statistics for children and teenagers are even more alarming'. After the numbers: 'We are turning childhood into a disease.' The author is editorialising at every break.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the author is using the statistics as proof that diagnosis is being applied far too widely. Words like 'dystopia', 'alarming', and 'turning childhood into a disease' are not neutral reporting — they are arguing that the numbers are evidence of a problem (over-diagnosis), not evidence of a real surge in illness.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Alarming' literally means causing alarm — it embeds a value judgement. 'Dystopia' is a society gone wrong (opposite of utopia). 'Overdiagnosed' is the key compound: 'over-' means too much; 'diagnosed' means labelled with a disorder. Together they say the diagnoses are real but excessive — not that the illnesses are.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Run each option against the paraphrase. The correct answer should match 'statistics presented as proof of excessive diagnosis'. Options that frame the numbers as proof of a genuine mental-health crisis, or as proof that more medication is needed, do the opposite of what the author is doing. The right option treats the numbers as evidence of over-labelling.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The author uses the numbers as ammunition for the argument that the U.S. is over-diagnosing childhood mental illness — the statistics are evidence that the system has gone too far, not that illness is genuinely surging. Insight in one sentence: when an author's adjectives around statistics are loaded ('alarming', 'dystopia', 'turning into a disease'), the statistics are being used rhetorically, not descriptively.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the rising numbers — 'tripled', '40-fold' — and conclude the author sees them as proof of a real mental-health crisis getting worse.
distractors.why_wrong: The author explicitly attributes the rise to 'wild and unwarranted diagnoses', not to a real surge. Step 2's framing words ('dystopia', 'overdiagnosed') tell you the author reads the numbers as artefacts of the system, not symptoms of the population.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that any author quoting peer-reviewed-sounding studies is taking them at face value as solid science.
distractors.why_wrong: The author quotes the Canadian study approvingly but uses it to MOCK the headline statistics ('the best predictor of an eventual ADHD diagnosis was a child's birthday'). The numbers are being deployed sceptically, not endorsed. Step 3 makes this explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'one in five adults takes psychotropic medication' and infer that the author finds the data unreliable, since the number sounds outlandish.
distractors.why_wrong: The author does not dispute the accuracy of the numbers — they accept them. What they dispute is the interpretation: that diagnoses reflect real illness. C confuses 'data is wrong' (which the author never claims) with 'diagnoses are wrong' (which they do).

host-2023-verb1-ELF-033.technique: Author-view detection through adjective scanning: when a question asks how the author treats the data, scan for the value-laden words attached to it ('alarming', 'remarkable', 'sobering', 'reassuring'). Those adjectives are the author's view in miniature. Trigger to memorise: stats + loaded adjective = rhetorical use; stats with neutral verbs = descriptive use.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Confusing 'data is accurate' with 'data is meaningful'. The author accepts the numbers but rejects the standard interpretation. The cure: split the question into two — does the author dispute the figures, or does the author dispute what the figures show? Here it is the latter.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The passage frames the ADHD surge as 'wild and unwarranted diagnoses of normal childhood behavior' driven by drug-company lobbying and over-eager clinicians — the recent picture, in the author's reading, is one of over-diagnosis, which matches C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the GENERAL PICTURE of ADHD that has emerged in recent years according to the text — what the trend looks like as a whole, not a single fact. With 'general picture' questions, the right answer summarises the author's overall reading of the situation, not one example inside it.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Three lines build the picture. 'In the past 20 years, the rate of ADHD diagnoses has tripled.' Then: 'A recent study of 1 million Canadian children proved that much of this shift comes from wild and unwarranted diagnoses of normal childhood behavior.' And the explainer: 'the best predictor of an eventual ADHD diagnosis was a child's birthday – the youngest boy in a class was almost twice as likely as the oldest to be diagnosed.'

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: diagnoses have tripled, but a big Canadian study showed that much of the rise reflects mis-labelling of perfectly normal kids. The clincher is the birthday finding — if being the youngest kid in a class doubles your diagnosis odds, the diagnosis is tracking developmental age, not real impairment. The picture is over-diagnosis, not a real epidemic.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Unwarranted' = not justified by the evidence. 'Mislabeled' = given the wrong label. 'Developmental immaturity' = behaviour that is just a child being a younger child. Together these words frame the recent picture as a labelling failure, not a medical discovery.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Match each option against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'normal kids are being mislabeled as having a disorder'. Options that say ADHD is being better understood, or that treatment is improving, run against the author's argument. The correct option captures the over-diagnosis framing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The passage's recent picture of ADHD is one of inflated and inappropriate diagnoses — kids who are simply young or active are being labelled, medicated, and turned into patients. Insight in one sentence: 'general picture' questions reward the option that paraphrases the author's framing sentence, not the most attention-grabbing statistic.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to see 'ADHD diagnoses tripled' and assume the picture is one of growing awareness — more kids identified means more kids helped.
distractors.why_wrong: The text immediately contradicts this reading: the rise is attributed to 'wild and unwarranted diagnoses of normal childhood behavior', not to better awareness. Step 2's middle sentence makes the author's interpretation explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'short-term side effects... anxiety, insomnia... stunted growth' and read the picture as one of medication risk being newly understood.
distractors.why_wrong: The side-effects sentence is one detail inside a wider argument about over-diagnosis — not the general picture. Step 3's paraphrase shows the headline is who is being diagnosed, not how the drugs behave.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line about 'no bright line separates a child with ADHD from one who is normally active', D feels right — the picture is scientific uncertainty about where to draw the line.
distractors.why_wrong: The blurred-line sentence is one nuance, but the author does not stop at 'we are unsure' — they go on to argue that the system is erring heavily on the side of over-diagnosis. D is too neutral; the author has a directional view, which Step 5's option-by-option match makes clear.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-034.technique: General-picture questions reward paraphrase of the author's framing claim, not the loudest fact. When you see 'general picture', 'overall view', or 'big-picture' in the prompt, hunt for the sentence where the author summarises their own reading — usually a topic sentence containing an evaluative phrase ('much of this shift comes from...'). The right option will be a near-paraphrase of that sentence.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Treating 'general picture' as 'sum of all the facts'. The correct answer is the author's INTERPRETATION of the facts, not a neutral inventory. The cure: identify the framing sentence first, then match options to it, not to the raw statistics.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The author concedes ADHD diagnosis is justified for the small minority (~3%) of severely impaired kids but argues today's medication-first approach is being applied carelessly to normal active children — a 'harmful diagnosis' producing 'unnecessary, expensive, and potentially harmful pills', which matches B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is ARGUED in relation to today's treatment of ADHD — so you are looking for the author's main argumentative claim about how ADHD is currently being handled. Argumentative questions reward options that mirror the author's evaluative stance, not options that merely restate facts.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. The careful version: 'ADHD can be a useful diagnosis, when made carefully, for the approximately 3 percent of kids whose impairment... is significant enough to require treatment.' The condemnation: 'ADHD can be a harmful diagnosis, however, when applied carelessly to normal, active kids who will likely fare better if left alone.' That 'however' pivots the whole argument.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the diagnosis itself is fine — for a small group of genuinely impaired children. The problem is the way it's being used today — sprayed across normal active kids who don't need it, with real side effects ('anxiety, insomnia, reduced appetite, weight loss, stunted growth'). The argument is: the tool is being misused, not that the tool is wrong.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Careless' = without enough thought; 'unwarranted' = unjustified by evidence; 'overmedication' = giving more drugs than needed. The pivot word is 'however' — it signals the author moving from a concession (3% is fine) to a critique (the other 97% are being mishandled). Pivots like 'however', 'but', 'yet' often mark where the real argument lives.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'medication is being overused and applied to children who don't need it'. Options that say treatment is generally appropriate, or that diagnosis should be expanded, run against the author's pivot. The correct option captures the over-prescription critique.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The author's argument about today's treatment is that medication is being administered too freely — to kids who would 'fare better if left alone' — with documented harms. Insight in one sentence: when an author uses a 'X is fine in case Y, but harmful in case Z' structure, the argument almost always lives in the Z half, and the right answer reflects it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'ADHD can be a useful diagnosis, when made carefully' and conclude that the author endorses current treatment practice — diagnosis is useful, so today's treatment must be working.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'useful diagnosis' line is the concession before the pivot, not the conclusion. The author immediately contrasts it with 'harmful diagnosis... applied carelessly'. Step 2's pivot sentence shows the argument's direction is critical, not endorsing.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'no bright line separates a child with ADHD from one who is normally active' and read the argument as a call for more research to clarify the diagnosis.
distractors.why_wrong: The blurred-line observation describes the diagnostic problem, but the author's argument is about practice today, not about future research. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the focus on over-prescription, not knowledge gaps.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the lines about pharmaceutical lobbying and $10 billion in sales, D feels right — the argument seems to be that drug companies are the villain.
distractors.why_wrong: Drug-company influence is one CAUSE the author names, but the argument about today's treatment is broader: it covers clinicians, parents, and schools, not just the industry. Step 5's option-by-option match catches the over-narrowness of D.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-035.technique: Argument detection via pivot words: when the prompt asks what is 'argued', scan for pivots like 'however', 'but', 'yet', 'although' inside the relevant paragraphs. The author's argument almost always lives just AFTER one of these pivots — the sentence that turns from concession to critique. Trigger to memorise: 'argued' in the prompt = find the pivot, take what follows.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: Confusing concession with conclusion. The author here grants that ADHD can be a real, useful diagnosis — that is the SET-UP, not the argument. The cure: when you see 'X can be A, but Y is B' patterns, treat the second half as the argument and the first half as the disarming move.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The author calls SCT 'the most ill-informed invention I have encountered in my 40 years' and warns it 'could potentially subject millions more children... to reckless diagnosis and treatment' — a more aggressive over-diagnosis risk than ADHD already represents, which is exactly what D says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the AUTHOR'S OPINION about SCT (Sluggish Cognitive Tempo) appearing as a suggested syndrome. With opinion questions, scan for evaluative phrases the author attaches to SCT specifically — adjectives, hedges, sarcastic framings.

steps.title: Locate the key phrases
steps.text: Three phrases mark the opinion. 'A wholly new condition... that could potentially subject millions more children, especially younger kids, to reckless diagnosis and treatment.' Then: 'SCT may be the most ill-informed invention I have encountered in my 40 years of evaluating diagnostic wish lists.' And: 'This can only be described as a public health experiment seeking to mislabel millions more kids as patients.'

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the author thinks SCT is even worse than the ADHD over-diagnosis they've been warning about. The scale is bigger ('millions more'), the science is weaker ('vaguely described', 'no scientific consensus'), and it targets a younger population. The opinion is: SCT will compound, not solve, the existing problem.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Reckless' = without thought of consequences. 'Ill-informed invention' = poorly thought-through new thing (calling something an 'invention' rather than a 'discovery' is a subtle dismissal — it implies SCT was made up, not found). 'Wish list' suggests the experts wanted a new diagnosis and then constructed one, rather than the disorder existing and then being named.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'SCT will be misused even more widely than ADHD'. Options that praise SCT as useful, or predict it will be rejected by clinicians, run against the text. The correct option captures the 'even worse than ADHD' framing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The author's whole article is about ADHD over-diagnosis, and they explicitly position SCT as a successor that will repeat the same pattern on a larger and younger population. Insight in one sentence: when an author who has been criticising X meets a proposed Y, and calls Y 'the most ill-informed' version of the same pattern, the opinion is always 'Y will be worse than X'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'a wholly new condition' as 'a useful new addition' — both phrases sound like the author is acknowledging a fresh contribution to medicine.
distractors.why_wrong: The author calls SCT an 'ill-informed invention', not a contribution. Step 2's second key sentence shows the framing is dismissive ('the most ill-informed'), the opposite of 'welcome addition'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'no scientific consensus exists' and conclude the author predicts SCT will be rejected by the field.
distractors.why_wrong: The author actually predicts the opposite — SCT 'appears to be racing toward clinical recognition'. Step 3's paraphrase keeps this in view: the author's worry is that SCT WILL spread, not that it will be blocked. B reverses the prediction.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on any 'new diagnostic category' question is to assume it will refine and improve the field — more categories means more precise treatment.
distractors.why_wrong: The author's whole point about ADHD is that adding categories has been used to widen the net, not to sharpen it. The 'wish list' language signals that SCT was constructed to capture more patients, not to differentiate them better. Step 4's vocabulary read catches this.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-036.technique: Opinion-on-a-named-thing scanning: when the prompt names a specific term (here SCT) and asks for the author's opinion of it, jump straight to the paragraphs that use that term and list every adjective and evaluative phrase attached to it. The right answer will fit the cumulative tone of those phrases. Trigger to memorise: prompt names X → grep for X in the passage → tally the adjectives.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: Reading 'new' as 'better'. Naturally, a 'new diagnostic category' sounds like progress — but in this author's frame, new categories are vectors for over-diagnosis. The cure: never assume positive valence on neutral-sounding nouns; let the author's adjectives set the polarity.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The vision scientist's experiment showed that even if El Greco had astigmatism he would have adapted to it within two days — so his elongated figures cannot be explained by uncorrected visual distortion and must instead reflect a deliberate artistic choice, which is what A says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what can be CONCLUDED from the text about El Greco. 'Concluded' means: what does the experiment in the passage entitle us to say at the end? Treat it like a logic exercise — find the experiment's result, then map it onto the original astigmatism hypothesis.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the conclusion. The experimental setup: 'a vision scientist persuaded a volunteer to wear a distorted telescope for two days straight... the copied squares were always picture-perfect, but the squares from memory... grew progressively shorter with time. By the end of the second day, she was drawing impeccable squares.' The author's takeaway: 'The conclusion was that even if El Greco suffered from astigmatism, he would have quickly adapted to it.'

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the test shows the brain corrects for distorted vision in about two days. So even if El Greco's eyes really did stretch images vertically, his brain would have re-calibrated within days and he would have drawn normal-shaped people. His paintings stayed elongated for decades — so the elongation cannot be a side effect of astigmatism. It must be a choice.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Astigmatism' = a defect in the curvature of the eye that distorts images. 'Elongated' = stretched lengthwise. 'Conclude' here means 'draw as the logical end-point of the evidence', not 'finish a sentence'. 'Deliberate' = on purpose, by intent — the opposite of accidental or pathological.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'the figures are by choice, not by visual defect'. Options that say his visual defect explains the paintings, or that he painted from memory, all assume the astigmatism THEORY survives the experiment. The correct option drops the theory entirely and credits artistic intent.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The text concludes that astigmatism cannot explain the elongated figures, since the brain adapts within days; therefore the figures must have been painted that way on purpose. Insight in one sentence: when a passage stages an experiment to test a popular explanation and the experiment defeats it, the right conclusion is always the OPPOSITE of the original explanation.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to scan the passage, see the long opening about astigmatism, and assume the text is endorsing the visual-defect theory.
distractors.why_wrong: The astigmatism story is the hypothesis being TESTED, not the conclusion. The experiment in the second half defeats it. Step 2's takeaway sentence — 'even if El Greco suffered from astigmatism, he would have quickly adapted' — explicitly closes the door B wants to leave open.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'the squares from memory were not always so' and conclude that El Greco's elongated figures came from imperfect memory.
distractors.why_wrong: The memory experiment is about showing how vision-brain adaptation works, not about how El Greco worked. The text never claims El Greco painted from memory rather than life. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the focus on adaptation, not memory technique.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the experiment as 'astigmatism debunked', D feels like the natural summary — the visual-defect claim has been disproved.
distractors.why_wrong: The text is more careful: it does not prove El Greco lacked astigmatism, only that astigmatism could not have caused the elongated figures (because he would have adapted). 'Proven incorrect' overshoots — the passage says the explanation fails, not that the defect is absent. Step 5's option-by-option match catches this overreach.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-040.technique: Experiment-defeats-hypothesis scanning: when an ELF passage sets up a popular explanation and then runs a test, the conclusion question almost always rewards the option that REJECTS the original explanation. Trigger to memorise: 'conclude' + experiment in passage = look for the option that walks the original story backwards.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Treating 'the visual-defect hypothesis fails to explain X' as 'the visual defect does not exist'. These are different claims. The cure: when you see options like 'proven incorrect' vs. 'cannot explain the feature', pick the more cautious option that matches what the experiment actually showed.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The article is written by a clinician who has spent 40 years watching diagnostic categories expand, opens with 'unless we soon come to our senses' and ends with 'it is time for policymakers... clinicians... parents to step up' — that is the cadence of a passionate protest against over-diagnosis, matching B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks how the BASIC CONTENT and TONE of the whole article are best described. With tone-and-content questions, focus on the recurring rhetorical moves — the opening line, the closing call to action, and the strongest adjectives — rather than any single fact.

steps.title: Locate the framing moves
steps.text: The opening hook: 'Unless we soon come to our senses, the United States may become a dystopia of overdiagnosed mental illness.' The mid-article evaluatives: 'wild and unwarranted diagnoses', 'turning childhood into a disease', 'the most ill-informed invention'. The closing call: 'It is time for policymakers... It is time for parents... It is time that we improve our school systems rather than medicate our students.'

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: this is an angry, urgent, evidence-laced argument that the U.S. is over-using psychiatric diagnoses on children. The content is over-diagnosis; the tone is impassioned and prescriptive. The triple repetition of 'it is time for...' near the end is a rhetorical hammer typical of opinion-page protest writing.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Passionate' = driven by strong feeling. 'Protest' = a public statement against something. 'Increasing use of psychiatric diagnoses' is the article's actual subject (the rising rates of ADHD, SCT, and other labels). Together 'passionate protest against increasing use of psychiatric diagnoses' is a near-direct description of what the article is and does.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'impassioned argument against over-diagnosis'. Options that frame the article as a neutral alarm bell, an accusation against parents, or a call for more research all miss the central target — the diagnostic system itself — and miss the protest tone. The correct option captures both axes (content = diagnosis-overuse; tone = passionate).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The article's content is the runaway use of psychiatric labels on children; its tone is heated and prescriptive. Insight in one sentence: tone-and-content questions reward the option whose two halves both match — many wrong options nail tone OR content but not both.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the alarming statistics — 'one in five adults', '81 percent of American kids' — and conclude the article is sounding an alarm about children's mental health.
distractors.why_wrong: The article is sounding an alarm about DIAGNOSIS, not about mental health. The author argues most of the diagnosed kids do not actually have a disorder. Step 2's framing words tell you the alarm is aimed at the labelling system, not at children's actual mental state. A nails tone but inverts content.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'It is time for parents to step up' and conclude the article is accusing parents and schools of failure.
distractors.why_wrong: Parents are addressed in the closing call to action, but they are not the target of the protest — the target is over-diagnosis and the drug-industry incentives behind it. Parents are recruited as allies, not accused. Step 5's both-halves check catches this content mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line about 'no one really knows what the long-term impact on the developing brain may be', D feels right — surely the call is for more research.
distractors.why_wrong: The article calls for restraint, second opinions, and watchful waiting — not for more research. Step 3's paraphrase emphasises action (legislate, opine, switch tactics), not investigation. D matches a small detail but misreads the article's overall thrust.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-037.technique: Tone-and-content questions reward two-axis matching: each option claims a tone AND a content, and the right one nails both. Trigger to memorise: when scanning options, split each into its tone phrase and its content phrase, and check both independently before picking. Many distractors get one axis right and one wrong.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Picking the option that matches tone alone. 'Alarm bell' (A) feels right because the article is alarmed, but the alarm is aimed at the diagnostic system, not at child mental health. The cure: never let a strong tone match override a wrong-content half.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The Tantalum paragraph says Anders Ekeberg named the element after the Greek figure 'because tantalum's oxide would not react with acids... unlike the compounds of most other metals' — the name reflects its chemical inertness, which is what B states.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is SAID about tantalum — so you are looking for a specific factual claim that the passage explicitly makes. With 'what is said' questions, the right answer is usually a paraphrase of a single sentence, and wrong options are usually plausible-sounding facts the passage never actually states.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The naming explanation is the clearest factual line: 'Swedish chemist Anders Ekeberg chose the name after he discovered the element tantalum in 1802, because tantalum's oxide would not react with acids, even plentiful amounts of very strong acids, unlike the compounds of most other metals.' The 'because' links the name (Tantalus = standing in water unable to drink) to the chemistry (oxide refuses to react with acids).

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: tantalum oxide ignores acids, even strong ones. Ekeberg saw that and thought of Tantalus — a man surrounded by water he could not drink — and named the element after him. So the name is a literary joke about the metal's stability: it sits in acid the way Tantalus stood in water, untouched.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Inert' = chemically unreactive. 'Oxide' = a compound of an element with oxygen. 'Compounds' = molecules formed by chemical combination. The Tantalus myth is the etymology hook: Tantalus was punished by standing in water he could not drink — and tantalum 'stands in' acid it does not react with. The pun is the link between mythological name and chemical property.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the paraphrase. The right answer should connect tantalum's NAME to its CHEMICAL STABILITY. Options that talk about supplements, ancient Greek knowledge of the element, or its natural occurrence in bone all add facts the passage never states. The correct option captures the name-stability link explicitly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The 1802 naming was driven precisely by tantalum's relative chemical stability — its refusal to react with acids. Insight in one sentence: 'what is said' questions almost always reward the option that re-uses the passage's own cause-effect link, not the option that extrapolates plausibly beyond it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to see 'screws used to hold together bones, artificial joints and plates in the skull' and assume the metal is used in supplements too — medical implants and medicines feel adjacent.
distractors.why_wrong: Implants are not the same as supplements or medicines. The passage names implants and industrial vessels but never says tantalum is ingested or formulated as a drug. Step 5's option-by-option match catches the implant-to-supplement slide.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that anything with a Greek-myth name must have been known to the Greeks themselves — name and ancient knowledge feel linked.
distractors.why_wrong: Ekeberg discovered the element in 1802 and merely BORROWED a Greek name for it. The Greeks knew the myth, not the metal. Step 2's date — 'in 1802' — is the giveaway: the Greeks could not have known a metal isolated 2000 years later.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'screws used to hold together bones' and infer that tantalum must occur naturally in human bone — otherwise why would it bond so well with it?
distractors.why_wrong: Tantalum is used in implants because of its inertness and strength, not because the body produces it. The passage never says tantalum is found in bone; that would be an extrapolation from compatibility to natural presence. Step 4's vocabulary keeps you anchored on 'inertness', not 'biological origin'.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-038.technique: Name-of-element questions reward the cause-effect link the passage explicitly draws. When a passage explains WHY something was named the way it was, the right answer almost always paraphrases the 'because' clause. Trigger to memorise: 'what is said about X' + an etymology paragraph = look for the 'because' or 'so' sentence.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Plausible-but-unstated extrapolation. Three of the four distractors here are scientifically reasonable but never written in the passage. The cure: before picking an option, find the literal sentence in the passage that supports it; if you cannot, the option is bait.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: Mayer-Schönberger argues that the Internet's perfect memory undermines our ability to forget, so that 'we may lose a fundamental human capacity to live and act firmly in the present' — the main focus is the negative effect online traces may have on future decisions, which matches B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the MAIN FOCUS of the 'Forget It?' text. Main-focus questions are asking for the single idea the paragraph organises itself around — what every sentence serves. Skim once, then ask: if I had to title this paragraph, what would the title be?

steps.title: Locate the spine
steps.text: Three lines form the spine. Premise: 'the ability to forget past events... is critical for decision making.' Reason: 'we can move on and make future choices without difficult or embarrassing episodes clouding our outlook.' Worry: 'when our decisions are tangled up in the perfect memory of the Internet... we may lose a fundamental human capacity to live and act firmly in the present.'

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: forgetting is useful — it frees us to make new choices. The Internet remembers everything we did, so our old footprint follows every new step. That could damage our ability to decide and act freely. The whole paragraph is about a harm the Internet's memory does to our future actions.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Recede' = move backward, fade. 'Safety valve' = a mechanism that releases pressure to prevent damage — here a metaphor for forgetting. 'Online footprint' = the trail of data a person leaves behind on the Internet. 'Firmly in the present' is contrasted with being weighed down by the past — so the worry is about freedom of future action.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the spine. The right answer should connect 'online memories' to 'negative effect on future decisions'. Options that praise the Internet's helpfulness for memory, or that frame online traces as distorting (rather than constraining), miss the worry. The correct option captures both halves — online traces + harm to future action.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The 'Forget It?' text focuses on how the Internet's perfect memory may harm our future decision-making by preventing us from leaving the past behind. Insight in one sentence: 'main focus' questions reward the option that names both the topic AND the author's evaluation of it; topic alone is not enough.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'perfect memory of the Internet' as praise — what an amazing capacity to remember!
distractors.why_wrong: The author frames the Internet's perfect memory as the PROBLEM, not the feature. Step 2's worry sentence makes the polarity unmistakable: this 'perfect memory' is what may rob us of human capacity. A reverses the author's valence.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'we can move on and make future choices without difficult or embarrassing episodes clouding our outlook' and read the focus as 'avoid past mistakes'.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says forgetting helps us avoid being WEIGHED DOWN by past episodes — not that the Internet helps us avoid repeating mistakes. C inverts the relationship: the Internet's memory is the burden, not the corrective. Step 3's paraphrase keeps this straight.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'memory' and 'online traces' as the key terms, D feels close — surely it is about online traces distorting our recollections.
distractors.why_wrong: The text's worry is not that online traces DISTORT our memories — it is that they preserve them too well and thereby block forgetting. 'Distorted' is the opposite shape of complaint. Step 4's vocabulary read ('firmly in the present') keeps the focus on action, not accuracy.

host-2023-verb1-ELF-039.technique: Main-focus questions reward the option that names topic AND the author's evaluative angle on it. Read the paragraph's strongest evaluative sentence ('we may lose a fundamental human capacity') and let it discipline your choice. Trigger to memorise: 'main focus' + an evaluative closing quote = match the option to the closing quote's polarity, not just its topic.
host-2023-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Picking an option that names the right topic with the wrong valence. A and C both touch the right subject but cast the Internet as helpful — opposite of the author's worry. The cure: extract the polarity (positive / negative / mixed) of the author's strongest claim and use it as a filter on the options.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The gap is part of the phrasal pattern 'can ___ us off' meaning to trigger an itch — the only option that produces a real English phrasal verb here is 'set us off' (to set someone off = to trigger a reaction), so the answer is D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Look at what surrounds the blank: 'Just thinking about something tickly, or even watching someone else scratch, can __ us off.' The blank is a verb that combines with 'off' as a phrasal verb meaning 'trigger a reaction'. This is a phrasal-verb cloze — the test is whether you know which English verb idiomatically pairs with 'off' to mean 'trigger'.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The sentence is about thinking or watching scratching causing an itch to start. The verb has to mean 'cause to begin' or 'trigger'. The pronoun 'us' sits between the verb and 'off', so the structure is 'VERB someone OFF'. Memorise that shape — it narrows the candidates fast.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: We need a phrasal verb of the form 'V someone off' meaning 'trigger a reaction in someone'. In English the canonical fit is 'set someone off' — meaning to make them suddenly start something (laughing, crying, sneezing, itching). The other candidates need testing against this idiom.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Set off' has two related senses: 1) to start a journey ('set off for London'), and 2) to trigger a reaction ('the smell set off her allergies'). Sense 2 is what fits here. 'Turn off' means to switch off or to disgust; 'let off' means to release or to excuse; 'put off' means to postpone or to discourage. None of those mean 'trigger'.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Try each in the sentence. 'Can TURN us off' = can disgust us — wrong sense; the passage is about triggering itch, not disgusting us. 'Can LET us off' = can excuse us — nonsensical here. 'Can PUT us off' = can dissuade us / postpone us — wrong direction (the passage says these things CAUSE itch, not prevent it). 'Can SET us off' = can trigger us — fits the meaning exactly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Set X off' is the standard English idiom for triggering a reaction (allergies, laughter, an itch). Insight in one sentence: phrasal-verb cloze items reward knowing which preposition each verb conventionally takes — guessing from general meaning is dangerous because three of four verbs may all sound 'close enough'.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to think 'turn off' is the most familiar phrasal verb with 'off' and try it without checking the sense — surely something can 'turn us off' to itching.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Turn off' means disgust or switch off; it would suggest the thoughts STOP the itch, not start it. The passage's logic is causal (thinking → itch), so the verb has to mean 'trigger', not 'deactivate'. Step 5's plug-and-test catches the polarity flip.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the visual similarity of short verbs ('let' is three letters like 'set') and treat them as interchangeable.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Let off' means release or excuse — neither sense fits a sentence about thinking causing an itch. Step 4's vocabulary inventory of phrasal-verb senses keeps you from grabbing a familiar-sounding mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'put off' is that it might mean 'put a thought to one side' — i.e. distract us into itching.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Put off' means postpone or discourage — the wrong direction. If thoughts of scratching 'put us off' the itch, the passage's whole argument (that itch is contagious) would collapse. Step 5 shows this by direction-checking.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-031.technique: Phrasal-verb cloze strategy: identify the preposition immediately after (or attached to) the gap, then ask which verb forms the idiomatic phrasal that matches the surrounding meaning. Trigger to memorise: gap + preposition = phrasal-verb test → check sense AND direction (does the trigger flow the right way?).
host-2023-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Mixing up 'turn off' (deactivate) with 'set off' (activate). Both involve switching, but they push opposite directions. The cure: read the surrounding clause for the causal direction (is X causing Y or stopping Y?) and pick the phrasal whose meaning matches that direction.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The blank follows 'current remedies are providing little __', so it needs a noun meaning 'help from suffering' that pairs idiomatically with 'provide little ___'. 'Provide little relief' is the standard collocation, so the answer is B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Look at what surrounds the blank: 'An estimated one in five of us will experience chronic itch at some point, and current remedies are providing little __.' The blank is a noun that pairs with 'provide little' and describes what suffering people get (or don't get) from treatments. This is a collocation cloze — which noun does English pair with 'provide little' in a suffering context?

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The context is chronic itch and current treatments not working well. So the missing word should be the thing that good treatment delivers and that bad treatment fails to deliver. In medical/health English the standard word for this is 'relief' — relief from pain, relief from itching.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: 'Provide relief' is one of the most common English collocations in health writing. The verb 'provide' habitually attaches to certain abstract nouns — relief, comfort, support, assistance — and 'relief' is the most natural fit when the topic is suffering or symptoms.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Relief' = the reduction or removal of pain, distress, or anxiety. 'Trust' = belief that something will work. 'Care' = the act of looking after someone. 'Support' = help that keeps someone going. All four are abstract nouns that combine with 'provide', but only 'relief' specifically describes the easing of a symptom — which is what failing remedies fail to deliver.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Try each. 'Providing little TRUST' — possible English, but it would mean people don't trust the remedies, which is a slightly different complaint (about confidence, not effectiveness). 'Providing little RELIEF' — natural collocation, matches the symptom-treatment frame exactly. 'Providing little CARE' — care is what providers offer, not what remedies provide. 'Providing little SUPPORT' — too generic; the passage is about itch, a sensation that needs RELIEVING, not supporting.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Provide relief' is the lock-step English phrasing for treatments easing symptoms; 'providing little relief' is the standard way to say a remedy isn't working. Insight in one sentence: collocation cloze rewards the option that produces the most idiomatic phrase, not just any grammatical phrase.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'remedies are providing little __' and think the issue is that people don't trust the remedies — trust feels like a thing remedies can earn or fail to earn.
distractors.why_wrong: Trust is something patients have or lack; it's not something a remedy 'provides' in standard English. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the collocation is off — 'provide trust' is a bureaucratic-sounding pairing that doesn't fit health writing.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that healthcare is about 'care', so any sentence about treatments must take 'care' somewhere.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Care' is what doctors and nurses provide to patients; remedies (drugs, creams) don't 'provide care'. Step 4's vocabulary distinction — care vs. relief — keeps you anchored on the right unit (the symptom-treatment relation).

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'support' because it sounds generic and safe — any treatment 'provides support'.
distractors.why_wrong: Support is too generic to fit the specific topic — chronic itch needs RELIEF (the symptom must ease), not 'support'. Step 2's read-around shows the topic is symptom severity, not chronic-condition management at a general level.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-032.technique: Collocation cloze strategy: when a noun gap follows a verb like 'provide', 'offer', 'give', or 'bring', ask which noun the verb idiomatically takes in that semantic field. Health writing strongly prefers 'provide relief'; legal writing prefers 'provide evidence'; business writing prefers 'provide support'. Trigger to memorise: gap = noun + verb in front = idiom test.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Picking a grammatically valid but unidiomatic noun. All four options can grammatically follow 'providing little', but only one produces idiomatic English. The cure: when in doubt, ask 'have I heard this exact pairing before in health writing?' If not, keep looking.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: The clause says 'Not all itches are immune-related, however, and antihistamines have proven to be __ for most forms of chronic itch' — the pivot 'however' signals contrast with the previous sentence (antihistamines work on insect-bite itch), so the gap must mean 'don't work', making 'ineffective' (C) the answer.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Look at what surrounds the blank: 'Not all itches are immune-related, however, and antihistamines have proven to be __ for most forms of chronic itch.' The blank is an adjective describing how well antihistamines work. This is a logic-pivot cloze — the word 'however' signals a contrast with what came before.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The previous sentence says antihistamines work on the immune part of itching (bug bites). The gap sentence pivots: NOT all itches are immune-related; for those non-immune itches, antihistamines have proved to be ___. The contrast demands a word meaning 'do not work' — antihistamines succeed on immune itch and fail on the rest.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The logical relationship is contrast through 'however'. Whatever sat in the previous sentence's positive slot (antihistamines block the bug-bite itch) must flip to its negative in the gap (antihistamines fail on chronic itch). So the gap is an adjective meaning 'failing to produce the desired effect'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Unproblematic' = causing no problems — wrong polarity (would mean antihistamines work fine). 'Superfluous' = unnecessary, more than needed — close but means 'not needed' rather than 'not working'. 'Ineffective' = failing to produce a result — exact polarity match. 'Sufficient' = enough to meet the need — wrong polarity (would mean they DO work).

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Try each. 'Have proven to be UNPROBLEMATIC' — would mean they cause no trouble, but the sentence needs 'they don't work'. Wrong direction. 'Have proven to be SUPERFLUOUS' — means redundant or excessive, but the issue is failure, not excess. Subtly wrong. 'Have proven to be INEFFECTIVE' — failure of effect, exactly what the contrast demands. 'Have proven to be SUFFICIENT' — opposite direction; would mean they DO work for chronic itch, contradicting the pivot.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'however' pivot demands that antihistamines fail on non-immune itch, and 'ineffective' is the only option whose meaning is failure-of-effect. Insight in one sentence: pivot-cloze items reward polarity matching — find the word in the previous clause that the pivot is reversing, then choose the option that genuinely reverses it.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to misread the pivot and think the sentence is praising antihistamines as a low-trouble option — they at least have no problems.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Unproblematic' is a positive adjective; it would make the sentence claim antihistamines are fine for chronic itch — the opposite of what 'however' sets up. Step 3's polarity check rules this out fast.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'superfluous' because it sounds technical and clinical, and feels appropriate for a science article.
distractors.why_wrong: Superfluous means 'unnecessary' or 'redundant' — close to 'ineffective' but distinct. Antihistamines for chronic itch are not redundant (no other drug is doing the job); they are simply failing to work. Step 4's vocabulary distinction catches the false synonym.

distractors.why_tempting: If you ignore 'however' and read the sentence as a continuation of the previous (antihistamines work), 'sufficient' feels natural.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Sufficient' would invert the contrast that 'however' is doing. The sentence is set up to TURN against the previous claim, not extend it. Step 5's plug-and-test catches D as a direction reversal.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-033.technique: Pivot-cloze strategy: when the gap sentence opens with 'however', 'but', 'yet', or 'although', the missing word almost always carries the opposite polarity of the key claim just made. Trigger to memorise: pivot word in gap sentence = flip the polarity of the previous sentence's main adjective.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Confusing near-synonyms with the exact polarity reversal. 'Superfluous' (redundant) and 'ineffective' (not working) both sound negative, but only one matches the specific contrast — drug fails vs drug is unnecessary. The cure: when you have two candidates of similar tone, read them back into the sentence and ask which one makes the surrounding causal logic land.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: The clause reads 'They are working to figure out what naturally activates these receptors in the hope of __ drugs that can deactivate them' — the researchers want to CREATE drugs, so the gap is a verb meaning to design or develop, making 'designing' (D) the answer.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Look at what surrounds the blank: 'They are working to figure out what naturally activates these receptors in the hope of __ drugs that can deactivate them.' The blank is a gerund (verb + -ing) describing what researchers hope to do TO drugs. This is a verb-selection cloze — which verb describes the act of creating a new medication?

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The researchers have discovered receptors involved in itch. They want to know what naturally activates them so they can CREATE a drug that does the opposite — deactivates them. The chain is: identify mechanism → create drug. The gap verb must describe the second step: building a new drug from scratch.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is creation. The researchers don't have these drugs yet — the drugs are the goal, not the starting point. So the verb must mean 'create' or 'develop', not 'improve', 'reveal', or 'enhance' (which all assume the drugs already exist).

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Improving' = making something existing better — assumes the drug already exists. 'Revealing' = uncovering something hidden — for drugs, this would mean discovering an existing molecule, not creating a new one. 'Enhancing' = strengthening or augmenting — again assumes existence. 'Designing' = planning and creating from scratch — fits researchers building a new drug from a newly understood mechanism.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Try each. 'In the hope of IMPROVING drugs that can deactivate them' — would mean drugs already exist and just need polishing, but the passage says receptors were 'discovered in recent years', so no drugs yet. 'REVEALING drugs' — to reveal is to uncover, but you don't 'reveal' designed pharmaceuticals into existence. 'ENHANCING drugs' — same problem as improving, requires pre-existing drugs. 'DESIGNING drugs' — drug design is exactly what researchers do when moving from receptor knowledge to a new molecule.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The researchers are designing new drugs from a freshly discovered mechanism — 'drug design' is the standard term for this stage of pharmaceutical R&D. Insight in one sentence: verb-cloze items reward asking whether the surrounding logic assumes the object already exists (then improve/enhance fit) or needs to be created (then design/develop fit).

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'researchers working to figure out... drugs' and assume any progress on drugs counts as 'improving' them.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Improving' presupposes drugs already exist. The passage emphasises that receptors are NEW discoveries and that drugs targeting them are still hypothetical. Step 5's existence check catches this.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the article's reveal-discovery vocabulary ('discovered new kinds of receptors'), B feels like the matching verb for the next clause.
distractors.why_wrong: Receptors are revealed; drugs are designed. The clause is past the discovery step and into the engineering step. Step 4's vocabulary distinction — reveal (uncover) vs design (build) — keeps the right verb for the right phase.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'enhancing drugs' because it sounds appropriately scientific — research enhances things.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Enhancing' is another improve-existing verb; it requires a starting drug to enhance. The passage frames this as a brand-new line of treatment, not a tweak. Step 3's relationship check (creation vs improvement) rules this out.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-034.technique: Verb-cloze strategy: when the gap is a gerund acting on an object, ask whether the surrounding logic presupposes the object exists or needs to come into existence. Improve/enhance/refine presuppose existence; design/develop/create presuppose absence. Trigger to memorise: verb-cloze + new-discovery context = pick the creation verb.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Picking a verb that sounds scientific without checking what it presupposes. 'Enhancing' and 'improving' are research-flavoured but assume a pre-existing drug. The cure: read the surrounding paragraphs for whether the object already exists — if not, only creation verbs survive.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The clause says the precuneus is 'distinctively responsive to itch' and follows with 'we can't pinpoint what it does in itch, but it's __ activated with itch as opposed to pain' — the contrast 'as opposed to pain' demands an adverb meaning 'only in this case', so 'uniquely' (A) is the answer.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Look at what surrounds the blank: 'we can't pinpoint what it does in itch, but it's __ activated with itch as opposed to pain.' The blank is an adverb modifying 'activated'. This is a contrast-adverb cloze — the phrase 'as opposed to pain' demands a word that draws a sharp distinction between itch and pain.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The sentence above the gap calls the precuneus 'distinctively responsive to itch' — itch sets it off, pain does not. The gap must echo that distinctive specificity. The contrast 'as opposed to pain' confirms it: whatever the adverb is, it must mark itch as singled out, set apart from pain.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is exclusivity / specificity. We need an adverb that says 'only in this case, not in the other' — i.e. only for itch, not for pain. 'Uniquely' is the canonical English adverb for that meaning ('uniquely human', 'uniquely responsive').

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Uniquely' = in a way that is one of a kind, only in this one case. 'Rarely' = not often, with low frequency. 'Consequently' = as a result, therefore. 'Hardly' = barely, almost not. Only 'uniquely' carries the 'distinctive to one case' meaning that matches the 'distinctively responsive to itch' setup.

steps.title: Plug and test each option
steps.text: Try each. 'UNIQUELY activated with itch as opposed to pain' — means activated only for itch, not for pain. Fits the contrast exactly. 'RARELY activated with itch as opposed to pain' — would mean itch rarely lights up the area, opposite of 'distinctively responsive'. Wrong polarity. 'CONSEQUENTLY activated' — would say the activation is a result of something prior, but no cause has been introduced. Logic doesn't fit. 'HARDLY activated' — same problem as 'rarely', it would say itch barely activates the area, contradicting the 'distinctively responsive' setup.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. 'Uniquely activated with itch as opposed to pain' is the adverb that captures the precuneus's specificity for itch. Insight in one sentence: contrast-adverb cloze items reward asking which option, when plugged in, produces a meaningful exclusivity between the two things being contrasted.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read scientific hedging ('we can't pinpoint what it does') and assume the writer is downplaying the activation — 'rarely' would fit a cautious tone.
distractors.why_wrong: The previous sentence calls the precuneus 'distinctively responsive to itch' — that's strong, not rare. Step 2's read-around catches the polarity: the writer is describing distinctive activity, not absence.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the previous sentence as a chain of cause and effect, 'consequently' feels like it could link the two.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Consequently' needs a prior cause clause; here none exists. The sentence is a description of WHERE itch activates, not WHY. Step 5's plug-and-test shows the logical scaffold isn't there.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'we can't pinpoint what it does' and think the answer must hedge — 'hardly activated' fits an uncertain tone.
distractors.why_wrong: The hedge in the previous clause is about FUNCTION, not magnitude — 'we can't pinpoint what it does' admits we don't know the role, not that activation is weak. The contrast with pain demands strength of distinction, not weakness of signal. Step 4's vocabulary distinction nails this.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-035.technique: Contrast-adverb cloze strategy: when the gap sentence contains 'as opposed to', 'in contrast to', or 'rather than', the adverb in the gap is almost always one that highlights exclusivity or specificity (uniquely, only, exclusively, distinctively). Trigger to memorise: contrast phrase + adverb gap = exclusivity adverb.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Mistaking scientific hedging for low-magnitude claims. The phrase 'we can't pinpoint what it does' is a hedge on function, not on signal strength. The cure: separate hedges-about-meaning from hedges-about-quantity, and let only the latter pull you toward 'rarely' or 'hardly'.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The opening paragraph calls the story 'a familiar story', lists generic types (king, royal advisers, intransigent pope), and only THEN names Lothar, Theutberga, Waldrada and Nicholas I — the structure shifts from pattern to particular case, so the paragraph's purpose is to frame these events as an instance of a common pattern, which matches C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the PURPOSE of the first paragraph — why the author opened the article this way. Purpose questions are about authorial strategy: the right answer names what the paragraph DOES for the article, not what it says.

steps.title: Locate the structural moves
steps.text: Two moves matter. Opening: 'It is a familiar story: a king dissatisfied with a loveless marriage that had lost its political value...' That sentence lists generic character types without names. Then: 'The king is Lothar II, great-grandson of Charlemagne; the two women Theutberga and Waldrada; the pope Nicholas I.' Only after the pattern is described does the particular case appear.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the author sets up the story as one example of a recognisable kind — a familiar template (unhappy king + manoeuvring advisers + immovable pope). The naming sentence reveals: oh, and the specific instance is THIS one. The paragraph's job is to make the reader see the case as part of a wider pattern before zooming in on its particulars.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Familiar story' = a story-pattern the reader has heard before. 'A dash of realpolitik' = a touch of pragmatic politics. The literary move here is called typing — describing characters by their roles before giving them names. It is used to signal 'this is one of those stories'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'this case as part of a common pattern'. Options that say the paragraph contrasts past and present, or emphasises uniqueness, or criticises kings, all miss the central move (typing the case as one of many). The correct option captures the pattern-framing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The first paragraph sets up the events as a familiar story-template, then drops the specific names into the slots — framing this particular scandal as one instance of a recurring shape. Insight in one sentence: purpose questions reward the option that names the rhetorical MOVE the paragraph performs, not the option that summarises the content.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the medieval setup and assume the article is contrasting medieval kings with modern times.
distractors.why_wrong: The first paragraph never mentions the present day; the contrast it draws is between THIS scandal and other medieval scandals like it. Step 2's structural moves show the contrast is type-vs-instance, not past-vs-present.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the colourful details (witchcraft, intransigent pope) and conclude the paragraph is emphasising what makes THIS marriage unique.
distractors.why_wrong: The phrase 'a familiar story' is the opposite move — it explicitly DEgenericises the events by calling them familiar. Step 2's first quote shows the paragraph is selling sameness, not uniqueness. The 'first royal divorce scandal in European history' detail appears later, but the opening's purpose is pattern-framing.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that medieval kings come off badly in modern retellings, so the paragraph must be criticising the king.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph is neutral about morality; it presents the king as one figure in a pattern, not as a villain. Step 3's paraphrase has no critical edge. D imports a tone the paragraph never strikes.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-036.technique: Purpose-of-paragraph scanning: name the rhetorical move, not the topic. Common moves include setting up a pattern, contrasting past with present, qualifying a previous claim, posing a question to be answered later, and providing a counterexample. Trigger to memorise: 'It is a familiar story' / 'It is a common scenario' = pattern-framing move.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Reading vivid detail as 'this is unique'. The opening contains witchcraft accusations and an intransigent pope — these are vivid but generic to the medieval-divorce genre. The cure: when a paragraph opens with a generic-noun list ('a king', 'a group of advisers'), assume pattern-framing until proven otherwise.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The text contrasts Lothar with earlier kings who divorced 'from a position of political security' and emphasises that Lothar 'was still a young man, fresh to the throne, whose grasp on power could not be taken for granted' — the crucial fact is that his hold on power was weak, which matches A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is ARGUED to be a CRUCIAL fact in the chain of events. So you are looking for the one piece of background the text claims made everything else play out as it did. With 'crucial fact' questions, scan for a sentence that explicitly says 'because of X, things went the way they did'.

steps.title: Locate the explanatory sentence
steps.text: The key explanatory pair: 'Earlier medieval kings – including Charlemagne – had extricated themselves from inconvenient unions without much fuss. They had done so, however, from a position of political security. Lothar, at the point of his attempted divorce, was still a young man, fresh to the throne, whose grasp on power could not be taken for granted.' The contrast between security (earlier kings) and shakiness (Lothar) is the explanatory hinge.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: other kings got away with quiet divorces because they were politically secure. Lothar could not, because he wasn't. His weakness — young, new, with a fragile hold on power — is the reason the divorce became a scandal, drew in his uncles, and eventually invited the pope to intervene. The whole chain hinges on his political weakness.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Grasp on power' = the firmness of someone's political control. 'Tread with circumspection' = act with great caution. 'Extricate from a union' = get out of a marriage. The contrast term is 'political security' — the thing earlier kings had and Lothar didn't. Whenever a passage uses a binary like 'they had X, but he didn't', the missing X is usually your answer.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'the king's political position was weak'. Options that talk about the marriage having no political value, about Theutberga's family opposing the wedding, or about prior divorce attempts, may all be partially true but are not what the text argues was crucial. The correct option names the king's weak hold on power directly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The text argues that Lothar's weak grip on power is what forced him to handle the divorce delicately, set off the chain of councils, papal involvement, and eventual collapse. Insight in one sentence: 'crucial fact' questions reward the option that matches the passage's explicit explanatory contrast — find the 'they had X, he didn't' sentence and the answer lives there.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'a king dissatisfied with a loveless marriage that had lost its political value' and conclude the marriage's political failure is the crux.
distractors.why_wrong: That phrase appears in the GENERIC opening (the 'familiar story' template) — it describes any medieval royal divorce, not the specifically crucial fact about Lothar. Step 2's explanatory sentence shifts to the unique element: his weak position. The text does say the marriage had value to begin with ('he had married her in the first place' because of her powerful relatives).

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Theutberga had powerful relatives. This was why he had married her in the first place' and assume her family is the crucial driver.
distractors.why_wrong: Her family's power is part of why Lothar had to be careful, but the TEXT'S argument is one level deeper — Lothar's weakness is what made her family's power matter so much. A king with a firm hold could have dismissed in-law pressure. Step 5's option-by-option keeps the focus on the explanatory layer, not the surface.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that a 'chain of events' implies a sequence of prior attempts at divorce that failed.
distractors.why_wrong: The text describes ONE divorce attempt that unfolded across years, not multiple prior attempts. There is no claim that Lothar had divorced or tried to divorce before. Step 3's paraphrase has no repeat-attempt element.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-037.technique: Crucial-fact scanning: look for an explanatory contrast (often signalled by 'however') where the author lines up two cases and says 'they had X, but this one didn't'. The missing X is the crucial fact. Trigger to memorise: 'crucial' / 'decisive' / 'pivotal' in prompt = look for the contrast hinge sentence.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: Confusing background features with explanatory drivers. Theutberga's powerful family is a background condition; Lothar's weak position is the driver that made the background matter. The cure: keep asking 'why does this fact MATTER?' until you reach a fact that itself doesn't need a deeper explanation.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The text says the boiling-water ordeal failed (the champion passed unscathed) 'for reasons which are still hard to explain, but which presumably reflect a lack of consensus at Lothar's court' — the implication is that people close to the king were not unanimously persuaded of Theutberga's guilt, which matches D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the text tells us in relation to Theutberga's TEST — the boiling-water ordeal. With this kind of question, locate the test, then look for the author's interpretation of what the test reveals.

steps.title: Locate the explanation sentence
steps.text: The test: 'Theutberga, or rather her champion, was compelled to plunge a hand into boiling water.' The outcome: 'the champion passed the ordeal unscathed.'
