The author's gloss: 'For reasons which are still hard to explain, but which presumably reflect a lack of consensus at Lothar's court, the champion passed the ordeal unscathed.' That gloss is the key — it tells us how to read the failed ordeal.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: Lothar set up the ordeal expecting it to prove Theutberga guilty. It didn't. The author can't say exactly why it didn't, but suggests the most likely reason is that not everyone at court was on board with the prosecution. Someone (the champion himself, the people running the ordeal, the witnesses) was sympathetic enough to Theutberga that the test was effectively rigged — or at least not rigged AGAINST her.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Ordeal' = a medieval physical test believed to reveal divine judgement (here, boiling water — supposedly God would prevent injury to the innocent). 'Champion' = a stand-in who undergoes the ordeal on behalf of the accused. 'Lack of consensus' = absence of unanimous agreement. The crucial phrase is 'lack of consensus at Lothar's court' — it tells you the divisions were INSIDE the king's circle.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'people in Lothar's court were not unanimously persuaded'. Options that say Lothar himself didn't want her proven guilty, or that Theutberga was cleverer than expected, or that her family prevented the test, all miss the actual gloss (lack of consensus at court). The correct option captures the divided-court framing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The text tells us that the ordeal's failure reveals a division within the king's own circle — not everyone close to Lothar believed Theutberga was guilty. Insight in one sentence: when an author gives an explicit 'presumably reflects X' gloss after an event, X is almost always the right reading; trust the gloss over your own theorising.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'for reasons which are still hard to explain' and assume Lothar himself secretly wanted the test to fail — perhaps he had cold feet.
distractors.why_wrong: The whole setup says Lothar ORCHESTRATED the ordeal to prove Theutberga guilty so he could divorce her. The next paragraph says he 'doubtless reckoned that his bishops would prove more reliable' — i.e. he was disappointed and looked for another route. Step 3's paraphrase makes the king's intent unmistakable.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'the champion passed the ordeal unscathed' and infer that Theutberga must have been smart enough to find an unbeatable stand-in.
distractors.why_wrong: The text gives no information about Theutberga's intelligence or her champion's choice. The gloss explicitly says the failure reflects court-wide lack of consensus, not personal cunning. Step 2's gloss sentence rules this out.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'Theutberga had powerful relatives' from earlier, C feels right — her family could have blocked the test.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says the test DID happen — the champion did plunge his hand into the water and emerged unscathed. The family did not prevent it; they may have influenced what happened, but the option claims prevention. Step 5's option-by-option catches the misreading.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-038.technique: Author-gloss trust: when the passage describes an event and then offers an interpretive gloss ('presumably reflects', 'doubtless reckoned', 'evidently believed'), use the gloss as the canonical reading. Trigger to memorise: event + 'presumably / doubtless / seemingly' = the gloss is the answer, not your reconstruction.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-038.pitfall: Inventing a more dramatic explanation than the text offers. 'Theutberga was cleverer than expected' (B) and 'Lothar didn't really want her guilty' (A) are both narratively richer than 'people at court were divided'. The cure: always prefer the author's own interpretation to a fancier one of your own.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: The uncles, Charles 'the Bald' and Louis 'the German', are described as 'descendants of the great Charlemagne – and with the dream of reuniting Charlemagne's fragmented empire never very far from their thoughts' who 'kept close eyes on Lothar's difficulties' — the implication is that they were scheming to capitalise on his weakness, which is what B states.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about Lothar's uncles. Implication questions ask you to draw the unstated inference from what IS stated. Locate the uncles in the passage and read every detail attached to them as evidence for the inference.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: The uncles are introduced as: 'The rulers of the kingdoms on either side of Lothar's realm were his two older uncles, also descendants of the great Charlemagne – and with the dream of reuniting Charlemagne's fragmented empire never very far from their thoughts.' Then: 'The king of the West Franks, Charles
, and the king of the East Franks, Louis
, kept close eyes on Lothar's difficulties.' Three details: descended from Charlemagne, dream of reunifying his empire, watching Lothar struggle.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the uncles ruled the kingdoms next door to Lothar's. They had a long-running ambition to reunite the empire under one rule — Charlemagne's old empire that had been split among heirs. They watched Lothar's troubles closely. Put those three together: they were positioning to absorb Lothar's territory if he stumbled, which is undermining behaviour by definition.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Scheming' = secretly planning, plotting. 'Undermine authority' = to weaken someone's power, often through indirect means. 'Kept close eyes on' is a watchful, opportunistic phrase — used of someone waiting for a moment to act. The literary technique is implication-through-juxtaposition: the dream of reunification is placed right next to 'watching Lothar's difficulties' so the reader connects them.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'the uncles were positioned to exploit Lothar's weakness'. Options that say the uncles cared about family reputation, that they were loyal to Lothar, or that they were piously religious, all run against the watchful-opportunistic framing. The correct option captures the scheming-against-the-nephew angle.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The uncles' Charlemagne-reunification dream plus their close watch on Lothar's troubles together imply that they were undermining their nephew's authority — waiting for the moment to claim his realm. Insight in one sentence: implication questions reward joining two adjacent details that the author has placed side by side; the unstated inference always lives in the join.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to think uncles of a king disgraced by a divorce scandal would be embarrassed for the family — family reputation seems naturally on the line.
distractors.why_wrong: The text gives no indication of embarrassment; it gives indication of opportunism. Step 2's framing — 'dream of reuniting Charlemagne's fragmented empire' — shows ambition, not concern. A misreads the emotional valence.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'kept close eyes on Lothar's difficulties' and interpret it neutrally — as concerned uncles watching out for their nephew.
distractors.why_wrong: Reading 'kept close eyes on Lothar's difficulties' as protective is the opposite of the author's intent — the previous clause about reunification makes the watching predatory. Step 4's vocabulary read catches the opportunistic register.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the article's later focus on the pope and on canon law, you might infer that the uncles too were driven by religious concerns.
distractors.why_wrong: Religion is not mentioned in connection with the uncles. The pope is a separate actor; the uncles' motivation is presented as dynastic-territorial, not religious. Step 5's option-by-option keeps the actors and motivations straight.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-039.technique: Implication-through-juxtaposition: when an author places two adjacent details about the same character (here: 'descendants of Charlemagne with reunification dreams' + 'watching Lothar's difficulties'), the implication lives in the connection between them. Trigger to memorise: 'X who Y, and Z' = read X+Y as the motive for Z.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: Reading 'kept close eyes' as protective concern. In opportunistic contexts, the same phrase signals vigilance for the right moment to strike. The cure: check the surrounding clauses for ambition signals (territorial, dynastic, professional); if present, the watching is predatory, not protective.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The text closes by saying the affair 'marked a milestone for papal authority' and that 'henceforth kings always had to reckon with the possibility of interference in their marital affairs' — the direct consequence is that European kings could no longer act independently, which matches C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what was the DIRECT CONSEQUENCE of the events discussed. Direct-consequence questions are about the immediate aftermath named in the passage, not about long-term ripple effects you might guess at. Look for the article's closing summary sentence.

steps.title: Locate the closing summary
steps.text: The article ends: 'This marked a milestone for papal authority. Even if Nicholas' immediate successors were not quite so forceful, henceforth kings always had to reckon with the possibility of interference in their marital affairs.' That is the explicit consequence statement — kings, going forward, had to expect papal involvement in their marriages.

steps.title: Restate in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: before the Lothar affair, a king's marriage was his own business. After Nicholas I excommunicated Waldrada and forced Lothar to take Theutberga back, kings of Europe had to assume the pope might step in. They could no longer treat their personal-dynastic affairs as fully theirs to decide. Their independence was reduced.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Milestone for papal authority' = a marker event that increased the pope's power. 'Henceforth' = from this point on. 'Reckon with' = take into account, prepare for. 'Interference in their marital affairs' = outside meddling in their marriages. The key phrase is 'always had to reckon with' — it means no king could ignore the possibility anymore.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk options against the paraphrase. The right answer should match 'kings lost full independence in matrimonial affairs'. Options that say the pope now decides ALL royal weddings, or that Lothar was tried and executed, or that the pope sided with Lothar, overshoot, hallucinate, or invert the actual outcome. The correct option captures the reduced-independence framing.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The direct consequence is that European kings could no longer be sure of acting independently in their personal-dynastic affairs — papal interference had become a real possibility. Insight in one sentence: direct-consequence questions reward the article's own concluding summary, almost word-for-word; do not over-extrapolate beyond what the text claims.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'a milestone for papal authority' and infer the pope now had full control over royal weddings — milestone sounds like total takeover.
distractors.why_wrong: The text qualifies the milestone: 'Even if Nicholas' immediate successors were not quite so forceful' — the pope did not gain absolute control. He gained the standing to intervene. Step 4's vocabulary distinction ('reckon with the possibility' vs. 'make all the decisions') keeps the reach calibrated.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'excommunicated Waldrada and threatened to do the same to the king' and assume Lothar himself was tried by the church.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says Lothar was threatened with excommunication but actually 'crumbled and unwillingly took back Theutberga' — no trial, no death sentence. He died in 869 still lobbying. B invents an outcome the passage explicitly contradicts.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'He eventually excommunicated Waldrada' and you mis-remember it as a verdict against Waldrada alone, D feels right — pope blames mistress, exonerates king.
distractors.why_wrong: The pope also 'threatened to do the same to the king' and pressured him until he 'crumbled' — the king was not supported by the pope; he was forced into submission. Step 5's option-by-option catches the supportive-pope misread.

host-2023-verb2-ELF-040.technique: Direct-consequence scanning: when the prompt asks about the direct consequence, read the article's final paragraph as the answer source. The closing summary almost always names the consequence in one or two sentences. Trigger to memorise: 'direct consequence' in prompt = match the option to the article's last evaluative sentence, not to the events themselves.
host-2023-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: Overshooting the strength of the consequence. The pope gained the right to interfere; he did not gain total decisional control. The cure: when matching options, prefer the option that uses qualifying language (could no longer be CERTAIN) over the option that claims absolute authority (the pope made ALL decisions).
